title: Door Cable Length Guide
summary: Use this guide to estimate realistic access-control door cable quantities using straight-line distance, routing factor, service slack, door count, and separate cable-run strategy.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: Door Cable Length Estimator converts a simple straight-line distance into a more realistic field cable quantity.
It accounts for routing path inefficiency, service slack, number of doors, and whether each door uses a single combined cable or multiple separate cable paths.
This tool is for planning cable quantity, pathway impact, and install difficulty. It is not a voltage-drop calculator or conductor-sizing tool.

sections.title: When to use it
bullets: Before creating cable takeoffs for access-control doors.
When comparing single combined cable runs versus multiple dedicated device cables.
When estimating rough-in material before final field routing is known.
When long hallway pulls, conduit routing, ceiling obstacles, or remote panels may add cable length.
When a door count increase may turn a simple material estimate into a pathway or labor issue.
When documenting why estimated cable quantity is higher than straight-line drawing distance suggests.

links.label: Lock Power Budget guide
links.note: Use this related guide when cable planning also depends on lock current, power supply sizing, or simultaneous unlock behavior.

links.label: Door Count Planner guide
links.note: Use this related guide when total controlled-door quantity drives cable takeoff scope.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These headings match the visible input labels on the calculator so the guide can be read directly against the form.

items.label: Straight-Line Distance (ft)
items.description: The approximate direct distance from the source location to the door before routing adjustments are added. This is the starting point, not the final installed cable length.
examples: Panel closet to door as measured from a plan view.
Power supply location to controlled opening.
Nearest riser or telecom room to the door location.
items.whyItMatters: Straight-line distance is usually shorter than the real cable path. The tool uses it as the baseline before adding routing factor and service slack.
items.commonMistake: Using straight-line drawing distance as the final cable quantity without accounting for walls, ceiling paths, conduit, risers, door loops, or routing detours.

items.label: Routing Factor
items.description: A multiplier that converts straight-line distance into a more realistic routed distance. It accounts for pathway inefficiency such as open ceiling routing, conduit paths, obstacles, risers, and indirect routes.
examples: Light: open ceiling or clean pathway with limited detours.
Normal: mixed field routing with typical walls, turns, and pathway offsets.
Heavy: conduit routing, obstacles, difficult paths, or long indirect routes.
items.whyItMatters: Routing factor is often what separates a clean estimate from a field miss. A route that looks short on a drawing may require much more cable once actual pathway conditions are considered.
items.commonMistake: Leaving the routing factor too low because the drawing looks simple, even when the install path requires conduit, wall fishing, ceiling offsets, or indirect routing.

items.label: Service Slack (ft)
items.description: Extra cable added at each door for termination, dressing, future service, hardware replacement, and practical installer handling.
examples: Slack at the door frame or junction box.
Slack at the panel, power supply, or controller end.
Extra length for service loops or hardware movement.
items.whyItMatters: Too little slack makes termination and service harder. Too much unmanaged slack can create messy pathways and wasted material.
items.commonMistake: Forgetting that every door needs practical termination and service length, not just the exact routed distance.

items.label: Door Count
items.description: The number of controlled doors using the same distance and routing assumptions. The tool multiplies the per-door estimate by this count.
examples: A group of office doors served from the same panel room.
A set of perimeter doors with similar routing assumptions.
items.whyItMatters: Small per-door estimate errors become large material errors when multiplied across many doors.
items.commonMistake: Using one average distance for all doors even when some doors are much farther away or require a different pathway.

items.label: Separate Runs?
items.description: Whether each door is treated as one combined cable path or multiple separate cable paths for devices such as reader, door position switch, REX, and lock power.
examples: Single: one combined composite-style cable or bundled path per door.
Multiple: separate cable paths for reader, DPS, REX, lock, or other door hardware.
items.whyItMatters: Multiple separate runs can multiply cable quantity quickly and may also increase pathway fill, labeling, termination time, and troubleshooting effort.
items.commonMistake: Estimating one cable per door when the actual specification requires separate reader, lock, REX, and door contact cables.

items.label: Number of Separate Cables (if multiple)
items.description: The number of individual cable paths used per door when Separate Runs is set to Multiple.
examples: 3 cables: reader, lock, and door contact.
4 cables: reader, lock, REX, and DPS.
items.whyItMatters: This value directly multiplies the per-door cable estimate in multiple-run mode. It can turn a manageable estimate into a major material and pathway load.
items.commonMistake: Leaving this number at a default without matching the actual door hardware specification.

links.label: Reader Type Selector guide
links.note: Use this related guide when reader selection affects cable type, interface, or wiring expectations.

links.label: Lock Power Budget guide
links.note: Use this related guide when lock wiring and power assumptions affect separate cable planning.

sections.title: How to interpret results
body: The results show how the straight-line estimate grows after routing factor, slack, separate runs, and door count are applied.

The most important output is not only total cable footage. Install difficulty and engineering insight explain whether the estimate is still a clean field pull or whether routing, pathway, and labor risk are becoming significant.
bullets: Routed Distance: straight-line distance after the routing factor is applied.
Routing Loss: the added percentage caused by routing inefficiency.
Estimated Run: the routed distance plus service slack for a single cable.
Estimated Total per Door: the per-door total after separate cable paths are considered.
Estimated Total Cable: total cable quantity across all doors.
Cable Density: how much the final per-door cable quantity grows relative to straight-line distance.
Install Difficulty: LOW, MODERATE, or HIGH planning pressure.
Design Guidance: practical next step based on estimated difficulty.

sections.title: Healthy / Watch / Risk interpretation
body: The status reflects installation planning pressure, not whether the cable can technically be installed.
A high cable quantity may still be possible, but it increases labor, pathway congestion, labeling needs, service risk, and chance of field changes.
bullets: HEALTHY: routing and cable quantity appear straightforward.
WATCH: routing is workable, but pathway efficiency, labeling, and slack discipline matter.
RISK: consider moving control hardware closer, splitting door groups, adding local power, or revisiting pathway strategy.

sections.title: Routing factor notes
body: Routing factor is the practical correction between drawing distance and real cable path.
A light routing factor may fit open ceilings or clean tray paths. A heavy routing factor fits conduit, obstacles, walls, offsets, risers, or difficult access routes.
When in doubt, use the factor that matches field reality, not the prettiest drawing path.
bullets: Open ceiling is usually easier than conduit-heavy routing.
Vertical risers and cross-building routes often need more margin.
Existing buildings usually need more routing allowance than new construction.
Security doors often need careful service loops and protected routing near the opening.

sections.title: Single vs multiple runs
body: Single-run mode is useful when a composite cable or combined pathway is being estimated as one cable quantity per door.
Multiple-run mode is useful when the design requires separate cable paths for reader, lock, door contact, REX, or other hardware.
Multiple-run estimates can grow quickly. That does not mean they are wrong, but it does mean pathway fill, labeling, termination labor, and serviceability should be reviewed.
bullets: Use Single when one combined cable assembly or single pathway estimate is appropriate.
Use Multiple when separate devices require separate cable counts.
Match the number of separate cables to the actual door hardware schedule.
Do not use Single just to make the estimate look cleaner.

links.label: Reader Type Selector guide
links.note: Use this related guide when reader interface or technology changes cable expectations.

sections.title: Field takeoff workflow
bullets: Group doors with similar routing assumptions instead of averaging the entire building into one number.
Measure or estimate straight-line distance for each door group.
Choose a routing factor that reflects actual pathway difficulty.
Add service slack per door, not just once for the whole project.
Select Single or Multiple runs based on the actual cable specification.
Multiply by the number of doors in that group.
Review high-difficulty groups separately before final material ordering.
Document assumptions so the estimate can be checked against field routing later.

links.label: Door Count Planner guide
links.note: Use this related guide when controlled-door quantity needs to be scoped before detailed cable takeoff.

sections.title: Red flags
bullets: Straight-line distance is used as final cable length.
All doors use the same average distance even though routing varies heavily.
Separate device cables are required but the estimate assumes one combined cable.
Routing factor is set low for conduit-heavy or existing-building work.
Service slack is omitted or only added once for the whole project.
Long runs are all home-run to one distant location when a closer controller or power supply may reduce field risk.
Cable quantity is estimated without checking pathway fill, tray space, sleeves, risers, or conduit capacity.

sections.title: Documentation checklist
bullets: Straight-Line Distance (ft) assumption.
Routing Factor selected and why.
Service Slack (ft) per door.
Door Count included in this estimate.
Separate Runs? selection.
Number of Separate Cables (if multiple).
Estimated Total per Door.
Estimated Total Cable.
Install Difficulty and Design Guidance.
Known pathway assumptions, risers, conduit routes, or field constraints.
Any door groups that need separate estimates.

sections.title: Common mistakes
bullets: Measuring straight-line distance instead of actual pathway distance.
Forgetting slack at the door and panel ends.
Using one average distance for doors that should be grouped separately.
Ignoring separate reader, lock, REX, and door-contact cables.
Underestimating routing factor in existing buildings.
Ignoring pathway congestion until cable pulling starts.
Treating the estimate as final without field verification.

sections.title: Assumptions and limits
bullets: Cable estimates are derived from straight-line distance adjusted by routing factor, plus service slack.
Multi-run mode multiplies the per-door cable quantity by the configured number of separate cable paths.
This tool estimates cable quantity and installation planning pressure, not voltage drop or conductor size.
Final quantities should be verified against actual routing, pathway conditions, service loops, spare capacity, installation standards, and the project cable schedule.
Outputs are planning aids and do not replace installer takeoff, manufacturer requirements, code review, or field verification.
